text.title: Git Server Workflow (Workspace ↔ Runtime) Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of `Git Server Workflow (Workspace ↔ Runtime)` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Files under /srv/<project>/current/ MUST NOT be edited live. Any live edit = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Live edits get clobbered on next deploy; the only record of the fix lives in operator memory.

rule.statement: The ONLY path source → runtime is `deploy.sh` (which calls rsync). Manual scp / cp / git pull in runtime = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Multiple deploy paths = state divergence; one path = one diff to review.

rule.statement: Every deploy MUST git-tag the source before rsync. Untagged deploy = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Without tags, 'what shipped at 14:23' becomes an archaeological dig; tags are the cheapest forensic record.

rule.statement: Source repos MUST live in `~/workspace/repos/<name>` (or `~/workspace/projects/<name>`). Source in /srv/ = REJECT.
rule.rationale: /srv/ is for runtime; mixing source there blurs the boundary and invites live edits.

rule.statement: deploy.sh MUST verify `git status` is clean (no uncommitted changes) before rsync. Dirty tree = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Deploying a dirty tree ships untracked changes that can't be reverted via git; first principle is `deploy = git checkout + rsync`.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume cycles and produce false confidence.
